title: Methodology
text:
Overview
The KROEG index is an index based on the Third Place Index (TPI) (https://thirdplaceindex.org) the number of businesses, companies and instances within a postcode area. KROEG stands for Kaart van Ruimtelijke Ontmoetingsplekken en Externe Gemeenschap in Dutch and KVK-based Ranking of Opportunities for Engagement and Gathering in English.
The KROEG index compares the amount and access to social and health infrastrucutres between postcode areas in the Netherlands. The derived dataset, dashboard and index can be utilized for inference and comparison between postcode area’s for research and policymakers to analyse access to certain infrasctructures.


Data
We drew upon data from the Dutch Chamber of Commerce to build the Third Place Index. We classified all registered entities in the Dutch Chamber of Commerce as belonging to one of the following sectors:

Food & Beverage: e.g, restaurants, cafés, or bars
Retail: e.g., supermarkets, department stores, or small business
Community Centres: e.g., libraries, youth centres
Cultural Venues: e.g, cinemas, bookstores, or theatres
Sport & Leisure: e.g., gyms, or attraction parks
Medical Services: e.g., pharmacies, hospitals, or doctors

The exact codebook can be downloaded on Github: Download Sector Classification Codebook.
The full KROEG index can be downloaded on Github:

Download KROEG Index as CSV File
Download KROEG Index as GPKG File



The Index
We calculated the number of Third Places of each of these sectors per PC-4 postcode. Then, we ranked the PC-4 postcode regions based on their number of Third Places in a given sector and calculated percentiles. This resulted in a score from 0 to 100. A value of 85 in the medical sector, for instance, means that this postcode has more medical services than 85% of the other postcodes. The data only contain this index and not the sum of Third Places per postcode as we could not export these numbers for postcodes with fewer than 10 companies due to the policy regulations of the CBS and of ODISSEI.
We also aggregated the sectors and calculated:

The total availability of Third Places
The total availability of Third Places excluding medical services
The total availability of Third Places excluding medical services and retail



Level of Urbanisation
Additionally, as the index — almost by definition — maps urban vs non-urban areas, we calculated each of these indices within the different levels of urbanisation. This means that the number of Third Places in a postcode is only compared to the number of Third Places in postcodes with the same degree of urbanisation. The urbanisation categories used by Statistics Netherlands (see see the CBS urbanisation categories) are:

Extremely urbanised: more than 2,500 addresses / km^2
Strongly urbanised: between 1,500 and 2,500 addresses / km^2
Moderately urbanised: between 1,000 and 1,500 addresses / km^2
Hardly urbanised: between 500 and 1,000 addresses / km^2
Not urbanised: less than 500 addresses / km^2

title: About
text:
Third Places
The term third place was coined by sociologist Ray Oldenburg in his 1989 book The Great Good Places. Third Places are spaces open to the public in which they can informally gather, mingle, and pass time, such as restaurants, cafés, bookstores, or libraries. The term “Third Place” stems from the distinction from “First Places” (the home) and “Second Places” (work and education). See Methodology for a full list of Third Places included in our definition.
The Third Place Index, following a publication from the US by Evan O’Neil, maps the availability of Third Places in the Netherlands. It gives a score to each area ranging from 0 to 100 describing how good the availability of Third Places is compared to other areas in the Netherlands. Low numbers indicate places with few Third Places and with higher danger of isolation while higher numbers indicate a wider availability of community building.
View the full description of the methodology


The Authors



Tim Florschütz
Tim Florschütz is a PhD candidate at the Pandemic and Disaster Preparedness Centre (PDPC), working on innovative infectious disease control with a focus on contact tracing. Background in infectious disease epidemiology and veterinary medicine.

LinkedIn






Aidan Kloots
Aidan Kloots is a Research Coordinator and Researcher with strong data science background who enjoys healthcare data and closing unfair gaps in healthcare. Over three years he worked daily in R with Shiny, complemented by Python and SQL. He built a Digital Twin Shiny app for PLGA microsphere production where he used Machine Learning from raw process data, trained Random Forest models to predict quality outcomes, and used Bayesian Networks to map probabilistic dependencies between process parameters and critical quality attributes. He models interactive dashboards for stakeholders and prioritise interpretability so clinicians can trust on model outputs.

LinkedIn






Till Hovestadt
Till Hovestadt is a data scientist, computational social scientist, and doctoral researcher at Nuffield College and the University of Oxford. With his work he aims to provide evidence-based predictions, policy recommendations, and analyses based on rigorous data science, machine learning, and statistical analyses.

LinkedIn





Acknowledgements
This project developed as part of the Summer Institute of Computational Social Science (SICSS) 2026 at ODISSEI Nederland. We want extend our gratitude to the organisers of the SICSS, most notably Tom Emery, Angelica Maineri, Javier Garcia Bernardo, and Tadas Bernotas. Last but not least, we want to thank the other participants of the SICSS ODISSEI 2026.

title: The KROEG Index
text:
This is an index comparing the amount of Third Places (see methodology across postcode areas in the Netherlands. High values mean a higher availability of Third Places than in other postcodes. The Kroeg index is constructed for a range of sectors. Below, you can choose the sector you want to visualise.
Choose an index:  All Third Places All Third Places without Medical Facilities All Third Places without Medical Facilities and Retail Stores Medical Facilities only Retail Stores only Food & Beverage Places only Community Spaces only Cultural Venues only Sports & Leisure Places only 
 Urbanisation:   Across the whole Netherlands Extremely urbanised Strongly urbanised Moderately urbanised Hardly urbanised Not urbanised
